# Review: `deepmind/287.lean` ‚Äî Erd≈ës Problem 287

## 1. Copyright Header

- [x] File begins with the **exact** copyright block (no leading blank line).
- [x] Year is exactly `2026`.

## 2. Import Statement

- [x] Exactly one import line, immediately after the copyright block (separated by one blank line): `import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports`.
- [x] No other imports are present.

## 3. Module Docstring

- [x] A `/-! ... -/` module docstring appears after the import, separated by one blank line.
- [x] Starts with a heading: `# Erd≈ës Problem 287`.
- [x] Contains a `*Reference:*` line with a markdown link to `erdosproblems.com/287`.
- [ ] Includes a brief plain-English description of the problem.
  - **Issue:** The module docstring contains only the heading, reference link, and bibliography entries (`[Er32]`, `[ErGr80]`). The plain-English problem description appears in the *theorem* docstring instead. The checklist requires a brief description in the module docstring itself.
- [x] Optional bibliography entries present: `[Er32]` and `[ErGr80]`.

## 4. Namespace

- [x] All declarations are inside `namespace Erdos287 ... end Erdos287`.
- [x] Namespace name uses `UpperCamelCase`: `Erdos287`.
- [x] No `open`/`open scoped` statements needed; none present. (N/A)

## 5. Custom Definitions

- No custom definitions in this file. (N/A ‚Äî all items pass vacuously.)

## 6. Theorem Statements

- [x] Uses the `theorem` keyword.
- [x] Main theorem is named `erdos_287` (snake_case, matching namespace number).
- [x] Name follows `snake_case` convention.
- [x] Has both `@[category ...]` and `@[AMS ...]` on the **same attribute line**: `@[category research open, AMS 11]`.
- [x] Has a docstring (`/-- ... -/`) describing the problem.
- [x] Docstring references source papers: `[ErGr80, p.33]` and `[Er32]`.

### `answer()` usage

The problem asks "Is it true that ‚Ä¶?" ‚Äî this is a **yes/no question** (Case 1).

- [x] Theorem type is `answer(sorry) ‚Üî P`.
- [x] RHS `P` formalizes the question as originally asked: for any finite set of distinct naturals >1 whose reciprocals sum to 1, there exist consecutive elements with gap ‚â• 3. This correctly captures the English statement.
- [x] Quantifiers (`‚àÄ`) go **after** `answer(sorry) ‚Üî`, not as theorem parameters.

**Formalization notes:** The use of `Finset ‚Ñï` naturally enforces distinctness. The condition `‚àÄ n ‚àà S, 1 < n` enforces that all elements are >1. The consecutiveness condition `‚àÄ c ‚àà S, c ‚â§ a ‚à® b ‚â§ c` correctly identifies adjacent elements in the sorted set. Since `a < b` is established, truncated subtraction `b - a` equals the true difference, so `3 ‚â§ b - a` is sound.

## 7. Category Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[category ...]` attribute present.
- [x] Value is `research open` ‚Äî a valid category.
- [x] `research open` theorem contains `sorry` in its proof body.

## 8. AMS Attribute

- [x] Exactly one `@[AMS ...]` attribute present: `AMS 11`.
- [x] At least one AMS subject number listed: `11`.
- [x] Numbers in ascending order. (Single number ‚Äî trivially satisfied.)
- [x] No duplicate numbers.
- [x] `11` (Number theory) is a valid AMS MSC2020 code.

## 9. Proof Bodies

- [x] Uses multi-line `by` / `sorry` format (`:= by\n  sorry`), which is acceptable.
- [x] No `sorry` in definitions (no definitions present).
- [x] No informal proof sketches for this open research problem.
- [x] No `native_decide`.

## 10. Variants & Multi-part Problems

- No variants present. (N/A ‚Äî all items pass vacuously.)

## 11. Code Quality

- [x] 2-space indentation throughout.
- [x] Unicode math symbols used: `‚Ñï`, `‚Ñö`, `‚àÄ`, `‚àÉ`, `‚àà`, `‚Üî`, `‚â§`.
- [x] No debug code (`#check`, `#eval`, `#print`, `dbg_trace`).
- [x] No `set_option` disabling linters.
- [x] Lines are reasonably short (all under 100 characters).
- [x] File ends with a single trailing newline.

## 12. Build Verification

- [ ] `lake build` ‚Äî not verified in this review.
- [ ] All five custom linters pass ‚Äî not verified in this review.

---

## Summary

**Status: PASS with minor note**

The file is well-structured and conforms to nearly all checklist items. The formalization correctly captures the yes/no nature of the problem using `answer(sorry) ‚Üî P`, the `Finset ‚Ñï` encoding is sound, and all attributes, naming, and formatting conventions are followed.

**One minor issue:**
- ¬ß3: The module docstring lacks a brief plain-English problem description ‚Äî the description appears only in the theorem docstring. Consider adding a one-sentence summary (e.g., "Is it true that any Egyptian fraction representation of 1 with distinct denominators >1 must have a consecutive gap of at least 3?") to the module docstring.

/-
Copyright 2026 The Formal Conjectures Authors.

Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
You may obtain a copy of the License at

See the License for the specific language governing permissions and

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erd≈ës Problem 287

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/287](https://www.erdosproblems.com/287)

[Er32] Erd≈ës, P., _Egyszer≈± bizony√≠t√°s K√ºrsch√°k egy t√©tel√©re_ (1932).

[ErGr80] Erd≈ës, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
theory_. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
-/

namespace Erdos287

/--
Erd≈ës Problem 287 [ErGr80, p.33]:

Let $k \geq 2$. Is it true that, for any distinct integers $1 < n_1 < \cdots < n_k$ such that
$1 = 1/n_1 + \cdots + 1/n_k$, we must have $\max(n_{i+1} - n_i) \geq 3$?

The example $1 = 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/6$ shows that $3$ would be best possible here.
The lower bound of $\geq 2$ is equivalent to saying that $1$ is not the sum of
reciprocals of consecutive integers, proved by Erd≈ës [Er32].
-/
@[category research open, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_287 : answer(sorry) ‚Üî
    ‚àÄ (S : Finset ‚Ñï),
      2 ‚â§ S.card ‚Üí
      (‚àÄ n ‚àà S, 1 < n) ‚Üí
      S.sum (fun n => (1 : ‚Ñö) / ‚Üën) = 1 ‚Üí
      ‚àÉ a ‚àà S, ‚àÉ b ‚àà S, a < b ‚àß (‚àÄ c ‚àà S, c ‚â§ a ‚à® b ‚â§ c) ‚àß 3 ‚â§ b - a := by
  sorry

end Erdos287

51-- [ ] Optional: bibliography entries for related papers.
52-

134:**How to decide which case applies:** Read the problem's English description (module docstring, theorem docstring, or source reference). If it asks a yes/no question ‚Äî "asked whether", "is it true that", "does there exist", "can one" ‚Äî use Case 1. If it asks for a specific value ‚Äî "what is", "find the minimum", "determine" ‚Äî use Case 2. If it asserts a fact ‚Äî "prove that", "show that", a direct inequality or bound ‚Äî use Case 3.
135-
136-## 7. Category Attribute

import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports

/-!
# Erd≈ës Problem 287

*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/287](https://www.erdosproblems.com/287)

[Er32] Erd≈ës, P., _Egyszer≈± bizony√≠t√°s K√ºrsch√°k egy t√©tel√©re_ (1932).

[ErGr80] Erd≈ës, P. and Graham, R., _Old and new problems and results in combinatorial number
theory_. Monographies de L'Enseignement Mathematique (1980).
-/

namespace Erdos287

/--
Erd≈ës Problem 287 [ErGr80, p.33]:

Let $k \geq 2$. Is it true that, for any distinct integers $1 < n_1 < \cdots < n_k$ such that
$1 = 1/n_1 + \cdots + 1/n_k$, we must have $\max(n_{i+1} - n_i) \geq 3$?

The example $1 = 1/2 + 1/3 + 1/6$ shows that $3$ would be best possible here.
The lower bound of $\geq 2$ is equivalent to saying that $1$ is not the sum of
reciprocals of consecutive integers, proved by Erd≈ës [Er32].
-/
@[category research open, AMS 11]
theorem erdos_287 : answer(sorry) ‚Üî
    ‚àÄ (S : Finset ‚Ñï),
      2 ‚â§ S.card ‚Üí
      (‚àÄ n ‚àà S, 1 < n) ‚Üí
      S.sum (fun n => (1 : ‚Ñö) / ‚Üën) = 1 ‚Üí
      ‚àÉ a ‚àà S, ‚àÉ b ‚àà S, a < b ‚àß (‚àÄ c ‚àà S, c ‚â§ a ‚à® b ‚â§ c) ‚àß 3 ‚â§ b - a := by
  sorry

end Erdos287
